channel.title: Comments for page "Document 48116-88 ("Son of the Earth")"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Document 48116-88 ("Son of the Earth")"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry, but there's something almost laughably ridiculous about seeing a blackboxed date with a footnote that says [DATA EXPUNGED]. I cannot take that with any degree of seriousness.

The rest, thankfully, is intriguing, and very redeeming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this, although I find the SCP-2000 reference a little blunt for such a short piece. Sort of a situation where the other piece carries a lot of the narrative heft, making the new material here seem pretty sparse.

I upvote somewhat hesitantly, but knowing that this references 2001: A Space Odyssy really blows it out of the water, in my mind. Very neat!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, it's not an SCP. Actually it's a reference to the movie "2001: A Space Odyssey" where in the end we see a "space child" whith its mysterious crystal eyes. I tried to express this idea: the astronaut, a "Son of Earth", turns from familiar, terrestrial, warm things to the cold unknown Space. To the Great Unknown. And after this moment he is no longer a Son of Earth, he is reborn and becomes a Child of Space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the last line referring to an actual SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ignoring the meat of the article for the moment, I was surprised by this for one reason. When we think about Foundation personnel, we think about doctors in sites, studying contained anomalies and coming up with ways to contain them better. We think about MTF crews, out in the field, hunting monsters. But time and again, articles will mention embedded agents, in governments, businesses, organizations and subcultures the world over. That's a lot of personnel, and we never hear their stories.

So that whetted my appetite and the payoff was absolutely worth it. :D This is great.

item.title: Re: Supposedly
item.content:encoded:
In my opinion the 'supposedly' that you're referring to is pretty much when it's used sarcastically. I think it would be fair to assume that in a clinical, scientific article people would not be using sarcasm and would be using 'supposedly' properly.

"accepted or believed as true, without positive knowledge" seems like a fitting definition.

e: However, I agree that "it is currently thought that" or something similar fits in more with The Foundation's non-presumptive nature.

item.title: Re: Supposedly
item.content:encoded:
OK, let me rephrase it.
First original: "The signal came from somewhere. It looks like it came from space station X, but we're not sure about it". Maybe I should have used "presumably" in this case.

Second original: "We believe no other telescopes detected the signal at all, although we did not yet check all the facilities - some hard-to-reach ones are yet to be checked". Maybe these facilities are distant and/or do not have embedded Foundation agents.

item.title: Supposedly
item.content:encoded:
Supposedly is an adverb that is used to indicate that an event may or not have occurred with the common implication that the writer does not believe the event occurred at all. My suggestion to make it sound more clinical (and please feel free to disregard if the use of this word was deliberate):

Original: "Supposedly, the signal originated from a space research station [REDACTED] …"

Suggestion: "The signal may have originated from the vicinity of the space research station…"

Original: "Supposedly, yours was the only telescope to detect this signal, although several facilities with low information availability remain to be checked."

Suggestion: "Your telescope is the only one that has detected this signal so far; we, however, have yet to verify with some of the facilities that are not up to speed with rapid information parsing."

Note: Although I tried to keep with the original formatting, I definitely added my own interpretation of the spirit of the sentences. Again, feel free to use or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Many thanks for the corrections! They was very helpful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
click
click

Should it be "by" or "via" if it's the radio telescope that did the receiving?

The message was received by the observatory via the telescope seems better to me

Attached find

I still think this reads a little strangely. After a bit of Googling it seems that both "find attached" and "attached find" are frowned upon. Perhaps "I have attached" would make it read better but it's probably only me having this issue.

Not immediately, as it took him some time to react and process the message. Will "promptly" do?

Promptly would work perfectly

Upvoted based on the changes made already and the explanations for the others. I'm a sucker for these 'stranded' stories, even if they are a tad unoriginal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks a lot! Edited about a half of those, but I'm not sure about the rest.

Questions
- Hide

via a radio telescope?

Should it be "by" or "via" if it's the radio telescope that did the receiving?

its proximity?

The proximity of Saturn and its satellites.

unless it's not the name of the space research station that's redacted

Exactly the case.

This may be intentional for purposes of brevity

I thought that's what lines like this one are supposed to look in official correspondence.

This sentence doesn't make sense to me, updated immediately? Will be updated shortly?

Not immediately, as it took him some time to react and process the message. Will "promptly" do?

Same here with the "did" - although I may be wrong

Emphasis on "did" as it refers to the "Seems like we got lucky" phrase earlier in this paragraph.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Line by line
.

██/██/19██ a message

This reads a little strange to me, I'd either add "On" to the beginning or make the date a heading somehow

observatory by a radio telescope

via a radio telescope?

a space research station [REDACTED]

Is this meant to be the space research station or just space research station, unless it's not the name of the space research station that's redacted

██/██/19██ the following emails

Same as before

are similar to what we use to communicate with Foundation satellites and probes.

I'd personally change "what we use" to "those we use" or something similar.

We were unable to pinpoint the location due to their proximity.

its proximity?

as there was no research conducted during that time

being conducted?

Attached find the decrypted message.

This may be intentional for purposes of brevity but if not I'd write "I have attached the decrypted message" or at least "find attached"

Log files were updated shortly

This sentence doesn't make sense to me, updated immediately? Will be updated shortly?

investigating into this incident

I'd say the "into" is unnecessary here

Although, if we did get lucky,

Same here with the "did" - although I may be wrong

They did not tell exactly what

Say exactly what? Tell me exactly what?

hug you through hundreds of millions of kilometers.

I don't know what to suggest for this but to me it reads strange

take it in stride

In my stride would be more traditional, even though in stride does work.

you no longer are there.

Are no longer there might read a bit better

Some or even most of these may be intentional and my personal preference is getting in the way but I thought I'd be specific as possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would be very kind of you if you could point us to this mistakes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No one is saying that, and this definitely appears finished, if short. Though I didn't spot any grammar issues myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see why translations should be exempt from the "don't post unfinished articles" rule and with the amount of grammar and style issues here I'd hope that it is unfinished.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eugh, there are problems with this. My upvote is for the note at the end. That gave me feels. The rest is… well, it feels like the earliest stuff on the wiki, and the earliest stuff on the wiki is not really the best.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems there more for realism than to provide a place to suggest a hidden meaning, like how it's normally utilized.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but there's one thing that's bothering me.
*checks footnote* [DATA EXPUNGED]
Why even bother adding the footnote then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Indeed, it was used improperly. The "a month ago" is what creates the issue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know it's present perfect, but I'm not sure if it was used properly.

The sentence was:

A month ago I've received a message from Mission Control

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short enough that I'm willing to overlook its flaws, which are mostly with the actual prose and most likely just a quirk of translation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't get a Marianas Trench vibe from this at all, because this took a much different approach to telling its concept than that one did.

This did its job fairly well. It felt sort of tacky and overlong at times with the hard sci-fi prior to the final note (it probably should have cut to the point sooner and more concisely), but it convinced me with the emotional weight at the end to overcome those problems. Maybe it's the fact that this resonates with me on an emotional level right now, but I fully enjoyed this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "I have received" is fine. It's present perfect tense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the corrections. You're surely right about the first and third notes, and I'll trust you on the second one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is way to similiar to this article for its own good.

+ Some grammatical stuff I caught, I didn't made any changes on the article in case I'm wrong: 
- Hide

facilities has received this message

have

A month ago I've received

I received

a sectet government

secret?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This tale is a translation, so we will be grateful for some grammatical and stylistic corrections.

Written by Blackbird5154 and iavev, translated by Gene R.
